card.intuition: Count how many times each distinct value appears, then use prefix sums to compute each value's starting position in the output. Scatter input elements into their correct positions using these positions — no comparisons needed.
card.analogy: Sorting a bag of marbles by colour: first count how many of each colour you have, then lay them out in colour order using those counts. No comparing individual marbles — just counting and placing.
card.time_complexity: O(n + k) where k is the range of values
when_to_use: Integers (or values mappable to integers) in a known, small range
As a subroutine in radix sort
When O(n log n) comparison-based sorting is too slow and k is small
gotchas: Only works for discrete, bounded values — not for floating-point or unbounded ranges
Stable counting sort: iterate the input right-to-left when scattering, decrement count[val] after placing
If values can be negative, shift all values by the minimum to make them non-negative

exercise.prompt: Implement counting sort for non-negative integers. Build the count array then reconstruct the sorted array.
hints: Enumerate the count array: for each (value, frequency), append that value freq times to the result
exercise.test_code:
assert counting_sort([4,2,2,8,3,3,1]) == [1,2,2,3,3,4,8]
assert counting_sort([1]) == [1]
assert counting_sort([]) == []
assert counting_sort([0,0,0]) == [0,0,0]
assert counting_sort([5,4,3,2,1,0]) == [0,1,2,3,4,5]
print('PASS')
